Salt Lake County Land Assessment

Automated analysis of land cover, land use, and burn severity.

Generated March 13, 2026 at 01:38 PM

Executive Summary

Executive Summary: Salt Lake County Land Assessment

Analysis of geospatial data from 1990 to 2024 indicates a consistent trend of urban expansion and land cover transformation within Salt Lake County. The most prominent change is the growth of developed land, which increased from approximately 21.3% of the total land area in 1990 to 33.14% by 2024. This transition is characterized by a steady rise in barren and impervious surfaces, reflecting the conversion of natural or semi-natural landscapes into residential, commercial, and industrial infrastructure.

Spatial patterns observed in Sentinel-2 imagery and Landscape Change Monitoring System (LCMS) data reveal a sharp geographic division. The central valley is defined by a dense urban grid and industrial zones, particularly in the northwest near the Great Salt Lake. In contrast, the eastern and western boundaries of the county maintain a high degree of vegetative cover associated with mountainous terrain. While the urban core has expanded, the surrounding natural areas remain subject to episodic fire activity. Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity (MTBS) data confirms that fire events are a recurring factor in the county’s landscape dynamics, with major records occurring periodically throughout the 34-year study period.

The cumulative data illustrates a landscape increasingly defined by human development. The expansion of impervious surfaces in the valley floor contrasts with the stable but fire-prone vegetation of the highlands. These findings provide a baseline for understanding the long-term trajectory of land use and the spatial distribution of environmental disturbances within Salt Lake County.

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Cover

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Cover

Between 1990 and 2024, the primary land cover trend in Salt Lake County was the steady expansion of Barren or Impervious surfaces and a concurrent decline in Grass/Forb/Herb cover. Impervious surfaces increased from 22.19% to 33.04%, surpassing grass-based cover to become the second-most prevalent class in the county. During the same period, Grass/Forb/Herb cover decreased from 23.73% to 15.10%. This inverse relationship suggests a consistent conversion of open or rangeland areas into developed surfaces.

Tree cover remained the most stable and dominant land cover type, maintaining a consistent presence of approximately 32% to 33% throughout the 34-year study period. However, Water cover experienced a notable reduction, declining from a peak of 6.61% in 1990 to 3.75% in 2024. Other categories, including Shrubs and various vegetation mixes, remained relatively minor components of the landscape, with Shrubs decreasing slightly from 4.27% to 3.08%.

Spatial analysis of the imagery reveals clear geographic concentrations for these transitions: * Urban Infill and Expansion: The growth of impervious surfaces (maroon/purple) is concentrated within the Salt Lake Valley, moving progressively from the central urban core toward the southern and western boundaries of the county. * Hydrological Change: The reduction in water cover is localized to the northwestern corner of the study area, showing a visible recession of the Great Salt Lake shoreline over time. * Topographic Constraints: Tree cover (dark green) is almost entirely restricted to the high-elevation terrain of the Wasatch Range in the eastern portion of the county. This area shows high spatial stability with minimal encroachment from other land cover classes.

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Cover Transitions

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Cover Transitions

Analysis of the Landscape Change Monitoring System (LCMS) data for Salt Lake County reveals a primary trend of expanding Barren or Impervious surfaces, largely at the expense of Grass/Forb/Herb and Shrub classes. While the Trees class remained the most stable land cover, maintaining approximately 28–30% of the total area across all time steps, it experienced a slight decline from 29.92% in 2010 to 28.44% in 2024.

The expansion of developed and barren land is the most consistent transition identified in the matrix: * Barren or Impervious cover increased from 17.8% in 2000 to 20.99% in 2010, reaching 23.76% by 2024. * The most significant contributor to this growth was the Grass/Forb/Herb class, which saw 4.2% of the total county area transition to Barren or Impervious between 1990 and 2000, followed by 3.62% (2000–2010) and 4.69% (2010–2024). * By 2024, the total area of stable Grass/Forb/Herb cover had declined to 11.67%, down from 17.73% in 2000.

Other notable shifts include a gradual reduction in Water surface area, which decreased from 5.44% in 1990 to 3.92% in 2010, and further to 3.52% in 2024. Transitions within the shrub categories were also present; Shrubs showed a decrease in stability, with 0.73% of the total area transitioning to Barren or Impervious surfaces in the final period (2010–2024). Overall, the data indicates a steady conversion of vegetated and hydrological features into impervious or barren land classes over the 34-year period.

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Use

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Use

Between 1990 and 2024, the most pronounced trend in Salt Lake County was the steady expansion of Developed land. This category increased from 21.55% to 33.14%, representing a total growth of 11.59 percentage points. By 2024, Developed land emerged as the primary land use category in the county, slightly exceeding Forest cover for the first time in the recorded period.

The increase in development occurred alongside a consistent decline in Agriculture and Other land uses: * Agriculture decreased from 9.58% in 1990 to 1.74% in 2024, a reduction of approximately 82% of its initial share. * Other land cover fell from 12.84% to 9.21% over the same period. * The rate of agricultural loss was most rapid between 1990 and 2010, with the decline slowing slightly in the subsequent decade.

In contrast, Forest and Rangeland or Pasture categories remained relatively stable. Forest cover began the period at 32.31% and ended at 33.12%, maintaining a consistent range with only minor inter-annual fluctuations. Similarly, Rangeland or Pasture showed minimal net change, starting at 23.7% in 1990 and concluding at 22.75% in 2024. These two categories combined continue to represent over 55% of the county's total land area, despite the ongoing urbanization of agricultural and miscellaneous lands.

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Use Transitions

Salt Lake County - LCMS Land Use Transitions

Between 1990 and 2024, Salt Lake County experienced a consistent expansion of Developed land, which grew from approximately 21.3% of the total area in 1990 to 32.75% in 2024. This growth was primarily driven by the conversion of Agriculture and Rangeland or Pasture. The transition to Developed land was most pronounced in the 2000–2010 interval, where 2.27% of the county's total area shifted from Agriculture to Developed and 2.52% shifted from Rangeland to Developed.

The Agriculture class saw the most significant reduction in footprint over the 34-year period. Occupying 9.46% of the county in 1990, it declined to 7.1% in 2000, 4.22% in 2010, and reached a low of 1.8% by 2024. While the majority of this loss is attributed to urban development, a smaller portion transitioned to Rangeland or Pasture (e.g., 1.02% of the total area between 2010 and 2024).

In contrast, natural land cover classes remained relatively stable: * Forest cover stayed consistent, fluctuating slightly from 31.93% in 1990 to 32.9% in 2024. The vast majority of Forest land remained unchanged across all time steps, with minimal conversion to other classes. * Other (including barren and water) decreased from 12.83% in 1990 to 11.06% in 2024, with the most notable transition being a 1.8% shift to Rangeland or Pasture during the 1990–2000 period. * Rangeland or Pasture maintained a significant presence, though it saw a net decrease from 24.15% in 1990 to 23.21% in 2024, as gains from former agricultural and "other" land were offset by steady losses to development.

Salt Lake County - MTBS Burn Severity

Salt Lake County - MTBS Burn Severity

Analysis of the Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity (MTBS) data from 1990 to 2024 reveals that fire activity in Salt Lake County is episodic, with major recorded events occurring in 1995, 2000–2001, 2003, 2010, 2012, and 2018–2019. The data indicates a shift in burn severity over time; while earlier events in 1995 and 2000 were dominated by Unburned to Low and Low severity classes, the mid-period events showed a marked increase in fire intensity.

The years 2003 and 2012 represent the highest severity peaks in the 34-year record. In 2003, Moderate and High severity classes accounted for a combined 59.02% of the burned area, alongside a notable 9.25% in Increased Greenness. The 2012 period recorded the highest proportion of High severity at 33.49%. In contrast, the most recent activity in 2018 and 2019 returned to a lower-severity profile, with 2019 consisting of 89.82% Unburned to Low pixels.

Spatial patterns observed in the animated imagery show that fire footprints are primarily concentrated in the mountainous terrain surrounding the Salt Lake Valley. Key geographic observations include: * Western Range: Frequent activity is concentrated along the Oquirrh Mountains, particularly in the central and southern sections. * Southwestern Quadrant: The 2010 and 2012 events show high-severity concentrations in the southwestern corner of the county, near the Traverse Mountains and Camp Williams area. * Peripheral Distribution: Fires are largely absent from the central valley floor, remaining confined to the wildland-urban interface and high-elevation slopes on the eastern and western boundaries.

Salt Lake County - Sentinel-2 Summer 2023

Salt Lake County - Sentinel-2 Summer 2023

The Summer 2023 Sentinel-2 imagery for Salt Lake County illustrates a landscape defined by a sharp contrast between the vegetated mountainous terrain and the developed valley floor. Vegetation represents the primary land cover by area, concentrated heavily in the Wasatch Range to the east and the Oquirrh Mountains to the west. The central valley exhibits a fragmented pattern where residential and commercial development is interspersed with managed green spaces, following a distinct north-south orientation along the I-15 corridor.

Spatial analysis reveals several distinct geographic concentrations and distribution patterns:

  • Vegetation Distribution: The highest density of biomass is located in the eastern canyons and high-elevation slopes. Within the valley, vegetation follows a "checkerboard" residential grid, with higher concentrations in established neighborhoods compared to the industrial sectors in the northwest.
  • Barren and Industrial Zones: Significant barren features are concentrated in two primary areas: the Bingham Canyon Mine in the southwest and the industrial tailings ponds and salt flats in the northwest. These areas show high reflectance and minimal vegetative cover.
  • Urban Morphology: High-density impervious surfaces are most concentrated in the Salt Lake City urban core and extend southward through the center of the valley. This central corridor is characterized by a mix of grey and tan tones, indicating a high percentage of built-up area.
  • Hydrological Interface: The northwestern boundary of the study area captures the transition to the Great Salt Lake. This zone is characterized by low-reflectance water bodies and high-reflectance mineral deposits (playa) along the receding shoreline.

The overall landscape composition reflects a high degree of topographic influence, with development strictly confined to the valley floor while the surrounding mountain ranges remain largely contiguous natural cover. The 2023 summer data shows a robust vegetative signature in the highlands, likely reflecting the residual moisture from the preceding winter's record snowpack.
